title: How to Work with Existential Questions That Arise During Journeys
body:
How to Work with Existential Questions That Arise During Journeys

You’ve probably been there. You’re in the middle of a psychedelic journey, feeling the walls dissolve, your thoughts scattering like leaves in the wind, and suddenly a question hits you. Not the usual “What’s for dinner?” kind of question, but the heavy, unshakable kind: “Who am I?” “Why am I here?” “Does any of this even matter?” Right?!

Existential questions like these are both common and challenging during psychedelic experiences. They can shake your foundations, leaving you vulnerable and confused - or, if you work with them carefully, they can open doors to new perspectives and healing. Here’s the thing: these questions don’t come with instructions. They just show up, loud and clear. And if you don’t have some strategies in place, they can feel overwhelming.

So, how do you work with these big questions when they arise? How do you handle the weight without getting crushed? Let’s break it down.

1. Accept the Question, Don’t Fight It

First off, don’t try to push the question away. I know it’s tempting. When that deep “Why am I here?” starts echoing in your head, your instinct might be to distract yourself, change the music, or try to think of something lighter. But here’s the truth: those questions are part of the process. They’re knocking because something inside you needs attention.

Imagine you’re on a boat in choppy waters. The waves are those existential questions. You can’t stop the waves, but you can learn to ride them. Accept that the question is there. Sit with it. Breathe into it. You’re not expected to have the answer right now. Just acknowledging the question is a powerful step.

2. Ground Yourself Physically

When your mind is swirling with big questions, your body often feels disconnected or overwhelmed. Grounding yourself physically can help anchor your experience back to the present moment. Something as simple as feeling your feet firmly on the ground, or your back resting against a chair, can remind you: you’re here, you’re safe.

One tool I often recommend is a weighted blanket for grounding (paid link). The gentle pressure can soothe your nervous system and help you feel more secure while you wrestle with those questions. It’s like a big hug from the universe when things get intense.

3. Write It Down, Even if It’s Messy

Sometimes the best way to work through existential questions is to get them out of your head and onto paper. Journaling doesn’t have to be neat or polished. It can be a scribble, a list of thoughts, or even a raw stream of consciousness. You might write something like, “Who am I if I’m not my job? If I’m not my thoughts? If I’m just this body floating in space?”

Writing can slow down the rapid-fire thinking and create some distance from the question itself. It’s like holding the question in your hand and inspecting it from all sides. Plus, when you revisit your notes later, you might find insights that weren’t clear in the moment.

4. Bring Compassion to Your Inner Critic

Those existential questions can sometimes trigger your inner critic. Maybe it starts whispering, “You’re lost,” or “You don’t have what it takes.” Look, that voice is part of the human experience. But it’s also not the final word. When that critic shows up, try to speak back with kindness.

“I see you’re scared. It’s okay. I don’t have all the answers, but I’m here with you.”

Compassion doesn’t mean ignoring the hard feelings or sugarcoating the experience. It means acknowledging your pain and confusion without judgment. You’re not broken for asking these questions. You’re alive.

5. Use Trusted Resources to Ground Your Understanding

You don’t have to figure this out alone. Books, podcasts, and videos made by people who’ve spent time thinking about consciousness and existential inquiry can help. One that changed how I see psychedelic journeys is How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link). Pollan’s writing is clear, grounded, and full of stories that make these big questions feel manageable instead of terrifying.

Another great resource is the work done by researchers at MAPS. They’ve studied how psychedelics can aid healing and increase meaning in life - that’s right, meaning, which is often what those existential questions are trying to get at.

6. Remember: You Don’t Have to Answer Them Today

Honestly, this might be the hardest part to accept. We want quick answers. We want certainty. But existential questions often don’t have clean, final answers. They’re more like companions on your journey rather than puzzles to solve. Know what I mean?

Think about the last time you asked yourself, “What’s the meaning of life?” Did you get a neat answer? Probably not. But just holding that question can open you up to new ways of seeing yourself and the world.

7. Create a Safe Space for Your Journey

Before you start your journey, set up an environment where you feel safe and comfortable. Soft lighting, cozy blankets, and quiet sounds can make a huge difference. Personally, I love using Sony WH-1000XM5 noise-canceling headphones (paid link) to block out distractions and create a bubble of calm where I can face my questions without interference.

Having a trusted sitter or guide can also help, especially if the questions get overwhelming. They don’t need to have answers either. Just someone who can remind you that you’re safe and held.

8. Use Breathwork and Body Awareness

When the mind races with existential questions, breathwork can bring you back to your body and the present moment. Try simple techniques, like slow deep inhales through your nose and long exhales through your mouth. Count to four on each inhale, and six on each exhale. Repeat.

Notice the sensations in your body. Your heartbeat, your chest rising and falling, the points of contact where you’re sitting or touching something. This can help calm your nervous system and prevent the questions from spiraling into panic or despair.

9. Check In With Your Values

Often, existential questions point toward a deeper need for meaning or purpose. When you feel lost, it can help to reconnect with your core values. What matters most to you? Is it kindness? Connection? Creativity? Adventure?

Write down three values that feel true today. Then ask yourself: How can I live in alignment with these values in this moment? Small steps matter. Maybe it’s calling a friend, going for a walk, or simply resting with intention.

10. Give Yourself Permission to Feel Confused

Look, feeling lost or confused doesn’t mean you’re failing. It means you’re human. Existential questions can stir up discomfort, and that’s okay. Sometimes the best thing you can do is acknowledge the confusion without rushing to fix it.

Think about a time when you felt lost but didn’t panic. Maybe it was during a tough breakup or a career change. What helped you then? Often, just giving yourself permission to feel what you’re feeling can ease the intensity.

11. Connect With Others

Isolation can make existential questions feel heavier. If you can, talk about your experience with someone you trust - whether it’s a close friend, a therapist, or a community that understands psychedelic journeys. Sharing your fears and questions can lighten the load and offer new perspectives.

If you’re looking for a community, places like the Psychedelic Society or online forums can offer support. Just hearing “Me too” can be incredibly healing.

12. Slow Down and Rest

After a journey filled with heavy questions, your mind and body might be exhausted. Rest isn’t just a luxury - it’s part of the healing. Take naps. Drink water. Eat something nourishing. Put on some calming music or light a natural beeswax candle set (paid link) to soothe your senses.

Rest helps your nervous system integrate the experience. Remember, the brain is remodeling itself during and after journeys. It needs time to catch up.

Wrapping Up

Existential questions during psychedelic journeys can feel like a storm. But they don’t have to be a storm you weather alone or with panic. By accepting the questions, grounding yourself, writing things down, and leaning into compassion, you can learn to hold what’s hard without losing yourself.

If you want more tips on integrating your journeys and working compassionately with difficult feelings, check out my article How to Integrate Your Psychedelic Experience. It’s packed with practical advice to help you move forward after the intensity.

For continual support and fresh insights on conscious healing, you can find me at kalesh.love. I’m here with you on this path, no matter how winding it gets.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
